1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
275	    \;\cong\;
276	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
277	  \]
278	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
279	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
280	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
281	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
282	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
283	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
284	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
285	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
286	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
287	\end{lemma}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
291	  \leanok
292	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
293	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
294	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
295	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
296	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
297	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
298	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
299	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
300	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
301	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
302	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
303	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
304	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
305	
306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
308	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
310	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
311	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto
313	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
314	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
315	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
316	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
317	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
318	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
319	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
320	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
321	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
322	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
323	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
324	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
325	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
327	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
328	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
329	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
330	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
331	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
332	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
333	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
334	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	
340	\leanok
341	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
342	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
344	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
345	  restriction map
346	  \[
347	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
348	  \]
349	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
350	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
351	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
352	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
353	\end{lemma}
354	
355	\begin{proof}
356	
357	\leanok
358	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
359	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
360	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
361	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
362	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
363	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
364	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
365	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
366	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
367	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
368	\end{proof}
369	
370	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
371	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
372	on their proof sketches.
373	
374	\begin{lemma}
375	
376	\leanok
377	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
378	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
380	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
381	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
382	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
383	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
384	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
385	  itself.
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	
390	
391	\leanok
392	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
393	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
394	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
395	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
396	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
398	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
399	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
400	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
401	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
402	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
403	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
404	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
405	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
406	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
407	  basic-open computation below.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{lemma}
411	
412	\leanok
413	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
414	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
416	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
417	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
418	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
419	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
420	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
421	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
422	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	
427	
428	\leanok
429	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
430	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
431	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
432	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
433	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
434	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
435	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
436	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
437	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
438	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
439	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
446	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
448	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
449	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
450	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
451	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
452	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
453	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
454	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
455	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
456	  \[
457	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
458	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
459	  \]
460	\end{lemma}
461	
462	\begin{proof}
463	  \leanok
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
465	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
466	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
467	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
468	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
469	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
471	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
472	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
473	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
474	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
475	\end{proof}
476	
477	\begin{lemma}\leanok
478	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
479	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
481	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
482	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
483	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
484	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
485	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
486	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
487	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
488	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
489	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
490	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
491	% is hand-proved per a.
492	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
493	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
494	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
495	  \[
496	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
497	    \;\cong\;
498	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
499	  \]
500	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
501	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
502	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
503	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
504	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
505	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
506	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
507	
508	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
509	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
510	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
511	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
512	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
513	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
514	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
515	\end{lemma}
516	
517	\begin{proof}
518	  \leanok
519	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
520	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
521	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
522	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
523	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
524	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
525	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
526	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
527	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
528	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
529	
530	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
531	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
532	  \[
533	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
534	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
535	  \]
536	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
537	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
541	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
542	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
543	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
544	
545	  \medskip
546	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
547	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
548	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
550	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
551	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
552	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
553	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
554	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
555	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
556	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
557	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
558	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
559	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
560	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
561	  an isomorphism.
562	
563	  \medskip
564	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
565	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
566	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
567	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
568	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
569	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
570	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
571	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
572	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
573	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
574	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
575	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
576	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
578	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
579	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
580	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
581	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
582	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
584	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
585	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
586	  movements.
587	
588	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
589	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
591	  \[
592	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
593	      \;\cong\;
594	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
595	  \]
596	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
597	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
598	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
599	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
600	
601	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
602	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
603	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
604	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
605	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
606	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
607	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
608	  \[
609	    \begin{array}{ccc}
610	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
611	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
612	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
613	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
614	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
615	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
616	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
617	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
618	    \end{array}
619	  \]
620	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
621	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
622	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
623	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
624	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
625	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
626	  \(\widetilde M\).
627	
628	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
629	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
630	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
631	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
632	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
633	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
634	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
635	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
636	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
637	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
638	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
639	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
640	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
641	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
642	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
643	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
644	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
645	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
646	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
647	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
648	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
649	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
650	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
651	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
652	
653	  \medskip
654	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
655	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
656	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
657	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
658	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
659	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
660	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
661	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
662	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
663	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
664	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
665	  naturality square for the inclusion
666	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
667	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
668	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
669	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
670	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
671	
672	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
673	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
674	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
675	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
676	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
677	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
678	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
679	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
680	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
681	
682	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
683	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
684	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
685	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
686	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
687	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
688	  % bump.
689	  \medskip
690	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
691	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
692	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
693	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
694	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
695	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
696	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
697	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
698	  needed for the affine lane.
699	\end{proof}
700	
701	\begin{remark}
702	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
703	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
704	  \begin{enumerate}
705	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
706	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
707	      \(R\)-module of sections of
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
709	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
710	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
711	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
712	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
713	      closed under isomorphism; hence
714	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
715	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
716	      needed for the affine lane.
717	  \end{enumerate}
718	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
719	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
720	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
721	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
722	\end{remark}
723	
724	\subsection{The pullback companion}
725	
726	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
727	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
728	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
729	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
730	
731	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints from the
732	per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison of
733	Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, packaged as a natural isomorphism of
734	right-adjoint functors. This packaging is realized by a Lean-side helper
735	\texttt{gammaPushforwardNatIso} and is no longer a separate blueprint node.
736	
737	\begin{lemma}
738	
739	\leanok
740	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
741	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
743	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
744	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
745	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
746	  % L1241--1256).
747	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
748	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
749	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
750	  % be affine schemes.
751	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
752	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
753	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
754	  % \begin{enumerate}
755	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
756	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
757	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
758	  % in the $S$-module $N$.
759	  % \end{enumerate}"
760	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
761	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
762	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
763	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
764	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
765	  \[
766	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
767	    \;\cong\;
768	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
769	  \]
770	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
771	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
772	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
773	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
774	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
775	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
776	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
777	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
778	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)
779	  of the same Stacks lemma of which the pushforward formula is part~(2).
780	\end{lemma}
781	
782	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
783	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
784	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."
785	\begin{proof}
786	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
787	  \leanok
788	  Pullback along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, on quasi-coherent sheaves, the
789	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
790	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
791	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
792	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
793	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
794	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
795	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
796	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
797	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
798	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
799	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
800	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong
801	  \widetilde{(R' \otimes_R M)}\). Naturality in \(M\) is the naturality of that
802	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on
803	  \(D(\varphi a)\) the localization \((R' \otimes_R M)[1/a]\) is
804	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
805	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)
806	\end{proof}

808	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
809	
810	The affine base-change lemma below reduces, via the locality criterion
811	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
812	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
813	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
814	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
815	Mathlib: the first is the base-change-of-the-base-change-map compatibility under
816	restriction to affine opens, the second is the coherence identification of the
817	abstract adjoint mate with the concrete cancellation isomorphism.
818	
819	\begin{lemma}
820	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
821	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
822	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.base_change_map_affine_local}
823	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
824	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
825	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
826	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
827	  % L920--926).
828	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
829	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
830	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
831	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
832	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
833	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
834	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
835	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
836	  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
837	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
838	  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
839	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
840	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
841	  by the affine-open locality criterion
842	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
843	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
844	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
845	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
846	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
847	\end{lemma}
848	
849	\begin{proof}
850	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
851	  Being an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules is, by
852	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, a property that may be checked on
853	  sections over the affine opens of \(S'\). Restricting the cartesian square along
854	  an affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) produces a cartesian square over
855	  \(U\) whose total space is the corresponding open of \(X'\); since affineness of
856	  \(f\) is preserved by base change, the restricted morphism is again affine and
857	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is again a tilde-module over the affine chart of \(X\) lying over
858	  \(U\) (this is where quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used). The
859	  base-change map is the adjoint mate of the unit of the
860	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, and one must verify that forming this mate
861	  commutes with the restriction; granting that compatibility, the general statement
862	  reduces to the affine--affine case. The required compatibility of the abstract
863	  adjoint mate with open restriction is not packaged in the pinned Mathlib, and is
864	  itself a base-change-of-the-base-change-map naturality statement; it is recorded
865	  here as a named obligation.
866	\end{proof}

868	\begin{lemma}
869	[Adjoint-mate identification with the cancellation isomorphism]
870	  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
871	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
872	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso,
873	  lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
874	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
875	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
876	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
877	  % L927--938).
878	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
879	  % to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
880	  % Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
881	  % $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated

883	  % Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
884	  % equality
885	  % $$
886	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
887	  % $$
888	  % as $R'$-modules."
889	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
890	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
891	  In the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
892	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
893	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), write \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) for the
894	  global-sections incarnation of the base-change map
895	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}).
896	  Transport \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) through the two affine dictionaries that identify its
897	  source and target: by the pullback dictionary
898	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} together with the pushforward dictionary
899	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, the source is identified with
900	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and the target with \(R' \otimes_R M\), both as
901	  \(R'\)-modules. Under these identifications \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) equals the concrete
902	  cancellation isomorphism
903	  \[
904	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
905	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
906	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
907	  \]
908	  (Mathlib's \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). Since
909	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis,
910	  this identification closes the affine case.

912	
913	\begin{proof}
914	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
915	  The base-change map is defined abstractly as an adjoint mate: it is the transpose,
916	  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunctions for \(g\) and
917	  \(g'\), of the morphism obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
918	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\). In the affine--affine case the four
919	  dictionary isomorphisms (the pullback dictionary
920	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} for \((g')^*\widetilde{M}\) and for
921	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F})\), and the pushforward dictionary
922	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} for \(f_*\widetilde{M}\) and for
923	  \(f'_*\) of the base-changed module) identify the source and target of
924	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) with the iterated tensor products
925	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and \(R' \otimes_R M\). The genuine remaining
926	  content is the coherence computation that unwinds the adjoint mate of the
927	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-unit through these four dictionary isos and shows that the
928	  resulting \(R'\)-linear map is exactly \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\),
929	  \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\). This mate-versus-
930	  concrete-map identification has no ready counterpart in the pinned Mathlib; it is
931	  the precise content that a later Mathlib bridge identifying the
932	  pushforward base-change map with the tilde of \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\)
933	  would supply. Granting it, the affine case closes because
934	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness.
935	\end{proof}

1014	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
1015	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine

1026	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
1027	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
1028	
1029	  \medskip
1030	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
1031	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
1032	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
1033	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
1034	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
1035	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
1036	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
1037	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
1038	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
1039	  \[
1040	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
1041	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
1042	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
1043	  \]
1044	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
1045	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
1046	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
1047	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
1048	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
1049	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is

1051	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
46	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
47	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
48	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
49	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
50	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
51	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
52	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
53	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
54	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
55	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
56	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
57	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
58	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
59	\end{theorem}
60	
61	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
62	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
63	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
64	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
65	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
66	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
67	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
68	% there exists a finite filtration
69	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
70	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
71	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
72	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
73	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
74	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
75	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
76	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
77	\begin{proof}
78	  
79	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
80	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
81	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
82	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
83	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
84	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
85	  finite \(B\)-module
86	  \[
87	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
88	  \]
89	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
90	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
91	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
92	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
93	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
94	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
95	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
96	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
97	  \[
98	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
99	  \]
100	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
101	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
102	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
103	\end{proof}
104	
105	\begin{theorem}
106	
107	\leanok
108	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
109	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
110	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
111	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
112	  % NOTE (iter-303, review): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
113	  % takes `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence / finite-type hypothesis, so it is
114	  % FALSE as stated (the blueprint correctly requires `\F` coherent and `p`
115	  % finite-type). Its `sorry` is therefore unprovable. The decl is NOT in
116	  % archon-protected.yaml, so the planner must re-sign it to add the coherent /
117	  % LocallyOfFiniteType hypotheses this statement requires before any prover is
118	  % sent at it. See .archon/task_results/Picard_FlatteningStratification.md.
119	
120	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
121	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
124	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
125	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
126	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
127	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
128	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
129	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
130	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
131	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
132	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
133	\end{theorem}
134	
135	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
136	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
137	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
138	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
139	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
140	% by restricting to a non-empty
141	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
142	\begin{proof}
143	  
144	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
145	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
146	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
147	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
148	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
149	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
150	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
151	\end{proof}
152	
153	\section{Mathlib status}
154	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
155	
156	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
157	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
158	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
159	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
160	generic-flatness argument consumes
161	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
162	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
163	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
164	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
165	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
166	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
167	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
168	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
169	are introduced in
170	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
171	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
172	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.
173

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
62	  % Polynomials" (read from
63	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
64	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
65	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
66	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
67	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
68	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
69	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
70	  %  the function
71	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
72	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
73	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
74	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
75	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
76	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
77	  %
78	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
79	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
80	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
81	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
82	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
83	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
84	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
85	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
86	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
87	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
88	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
89	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
90	  III.5.2.}
91	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
92	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
93	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
94	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
95	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
96	  \[
97	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
98	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
99	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
100	  \]
101	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
102	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
103	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
104	  well-defined on connected components.
105	\end{definition}
106	
107	\section{The Quot functor}
108	\label{sec:quot_functor}
109	
110	\begin{definition}\leanok
111	[Quot functor]
112	  \label{def:quot_functor}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
114	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial}
115	
116	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
117	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
118	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
119	  % + L481-L498).
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
121	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
122	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
123	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
124	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
125	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
126	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
127	  %
128	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
129	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
130	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
131	  %
132	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
133	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
134	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
135	  %
136	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
137	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
138	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
139	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
140	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
141	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
142	  %  well-defined, which gives
143	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
144	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
145	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
146	  %  [...]
147	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
148	  %  as a co-product
149	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
150	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
151	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
152	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
153	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
154	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
155	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
157	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
158	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
159	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
160	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
161	  \[
162	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
163	  \]
164	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
165	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
166	  \begin{enumerate}
167	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
168	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
169	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
170	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
171	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
172	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
173	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
174	      \(\Phi\).
175	  \end{enumerate}
176	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
177	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
178	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
179	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
180	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
181	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
182	\end{definition}

184	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
185	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
186	
187	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
188	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
189	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
190	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
191	
192	\begin{definition}\leanok
193	[Grassmannian scheme]
194	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
196	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
197	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
198	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
199	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
200	  % + L773-L850).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
203	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
204	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
205	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
206	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
207	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
208	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
209	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
210	  %  contravariant functor
211	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
212	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
213	  %  set of all equivalence classes
214	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
215	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
216	  %  [...]
217	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
218	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
219	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
220	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
221	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
222	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
223	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
224	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
225	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
226	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
228	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
229	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
230	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
231	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
232	  \[
233	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
234	    \qquad
235	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
236	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
237	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
238	  \]
239	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
240	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
241	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
242	\end{definition}
